data.description:
# Workspace-switch performance — umbrella roadmap

## Problem

Long-standing perf issue: workspace switches commonly take 1–6 s with outliers up to 90 s under heavy load. A live `sample(1)` showed ~64% of main thread spent in `-[NSView _layoutSubtreeWithOldSize:]` recursion ~30 levels deep — AppKit Auto Layout walking the full bonsplit pane tree of every mounted workspace on every switch, even though only one is visible.

Per-switch timeline (pre-fix) at 7 workspaces / ~9 surfaces:

| dt | event |
|---|---|
| 0 ms | `selectedTabId` setter (sync, innocent — sub-ms) |
| 1,530 ms | `ws.view.selectedChange` (SwiftUI body cascade fully drained) |
| 1,672 ms | last `ws.swiftui.update` for surfaces |
| ~3,668 ms | `ws.unfocus.defer` — silent gap = AppKit layout cascade #1 |
| ~5,118 ms | `ws.select.asyncDone` — silent gap = AppKit layout cascade #2 |

## Roadmap

### Phase 0 — OSSignpost instrumentation ✅ DONE
Always-on (DEBUG and Release) `os_signpost` facade so Instruments can graph the switch path; release-safe Sentry breadcrumb `workspace.switch.complete` with `dt_ms`. Phase events at every existing `ws.*` dlog site.

**Shipped in:** PR #127 / branch `perf/workspace-switch-instrumentation` / commit `4ac704b8`.

### Phase 1 — Hide off-screen workspaces from AppKit layout ✅ DONE
`AppKitHiddenWrapper` (`NSViewControllerRepresentable` hosting `NSHostingController<Content>`, toggling `host.view.isHidden`) wraps every `WorkspaceContentView` in `ContentView.terminalContent`. AppKit's `_layoutSubtreeWithOldSize:` short-circuits at hidden subviews, so off-screen workspaces' subtrees are skipped entirely. SwiftUI subtree preserved; surfaces don't dismount/remount.

**Shipped in:** Same commit as Phase 0.

**Measured impact** (11 workspaces / ~20 agents loaded):

| Metric | Baseline | Phase 0+1 | Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| `handoff.start` dt (SwiftUI cascade) | ~1,500 ms | 17–77 ms | ~30–80× |
| `asyncDone` median | ~1,000 ms | ~325 ms | ~3× |
| `asyncDone` p95 | ~6,000 ms | ~1,200 ms | ~5× |
| `asyncDone` worst seen | 90,790 ms | 2,426 ms | tail eliminated |

### Phase 2 — Eliminate the deferred portal bind 🔄 IN PROGRESS
Currently surfaces mount in SwiftUI before their host containers are in an NSWindow → `ws.hostState.deferBind reason=hostNoWindow` fires for each → portal bind is deferred to `onDidMoveToWindow` callback (GhosttyTerminalView.swift:9344-9370 / 9462-9469) → when the deferred bind fires after the first AppKit layout cascade, it triggers a second one.

Two candidate fixes:
- **(a)** Pre-attach host containers eagerly so they're in the window before SwiftUI mounts the surface (synchronous bind in `updateNSView`).
- **(b)** Defer SwiftUI mount until host is in window via a guard at the surface boundary.

Implementation agent active in c11 surface (pane:52 / `phase2-impl`) at the time of writing.

**Expected impact:** collapses the second AppKit layout cascade. On heavy switches this is the 700–1,500 ms `last swiftui.update → unfocus.defer` gap. Could roughly halve heavy-switch `asyncDone`.

### Phase 3 — Defer non-essential async-block work past the visible flip
Today the queued `DispatchQueue.main.async` block in `selectedTabId.didSet` runs `focusSelectedTabPanel` + `updateWindowTitleForSelectedTab` + `markFocusedPanelReadIfActive` together. `panel.focus()` invalidates layout, triggering a third cascade (the `unfocus.defer → asyncDone` gap of 150–350 ms).

**Fix:** split into two queued blocks. Block 1 (immediate): focus only — needed for input routing. Block 2 (next runloop tick after first frame paint): title update + read mark + any other side-effects.

**Expected impact:** moves the third cascade out of the user-perceived switch window, even if the underlying work cost stays. Smaller, lower-risk than Phase 2. May be moot if Phase 2 already collapses both remaining cascades; decide after Phase 2 lands.

### Phase 4 — Narrow `terminalContent`'s ForEach observation
Phase 1 stops AppKit from laying out invisible workspaces, but the SwiftUI body of every mounted `WorkspaceContentView` still re-evaluates on every `selectedTabId` flip. The `ForEach(mountedWorkspaces)` in `ContentView.terminalContent` re-creates prop bindings (`isWorkspaceVisible`, `isWorkspaceInputActive`, `workspacePortalPriority`) for each mounted workspace whenever any of them changes. So 7 mounted workspaces' bodies + their pane trees + every surface's `updateNSView` all re-evaluate per switch.

**Fix:** hoist per-workspace presentation state into per-workspace `ObservableObject`s (e.g., `WorkspacePresentationStore`). Each `WorkspaceContentView` observes only its own slice. The ForEach iteration only varies on workspace identity; props don't change. Workspaces whose state didn't change skip body re-eval, skip `updateNSView`, skip the whole subtree's SwiftUI cascade.

**Expected impact:** cuts the SwiftUI body-eval cascade from O(mounted) to O(2) — the workspace going inactive + the one going active. Probably 50–150 ms on heavy switches. Also reduces ambient main-thread work during agent activity.

**Effort/risk:** Medium / medium. Localized to ContentView + WorkspaceContentView. State propagation must preserve handoff-during-retiring and background-prime semantics that `terminalContent` currently encodes inline.

### Phase 5 — Narrow `@EnvironmentObject TabManager` into smaller stores
`TabManager` is held as `@EnvironmentObject` in ~4 root views (`ContentView`, sidebar root, `NotificationsPage`, `SidebarEmptyArea`). Any `@Published` change in `TabManager` — `selectedTabId`, `tabs`, `isWorkspaceCycleHot`, `pendingBackgroundWorkspaceLoadIds`, status mutations, history mutations — re-evaluates **all four** root views, regardless of which property they actually read. This is the structural reason ambient activity (panel-title coalesce, status updates, agent output triggering badge changes) feels heavy even when nothing visible is changing.

**Fix:** split `TabManager`'s published surface into smaller `ObservableObject`s — `WorkspaceSelectionStore` (`selectedTabId`), `TabsListStore` (`tabs`), `WorkspaceStatusStore` (status/badges), `WorkspaceCycleStore` (cycle/hot state). Each root view observes only the slice it needs. `TabManager` itself stays as the orchestrator but stops being a single broadcast source.

**Expected impact:** the single biggest fix for *ambient* "system feels sluggish" (separate from switching). Every TabManager publish stops being a 4-way broadcast. Pays off across the entire app. Likely cuts SwiftUI body-eval volume by 5–10× under heavy agent activity.

**Effort/risk:** High / medium. `TabManager` is 3,000+ lines used everywhere; this is a careful refactor, not a fix. But mechanical (move `@Published` markers; introduce sub-stores; thread through environment) — no new logic. Ideally spread over 2–3 PRs.

### Phase 6 — Bonsplit nesting reduction
The 30-level `_layoutSubtreeWithOldSize:` recursion comes from bonsplit. Each split level adds ~5 NSView wrappers (`NSSplitView` + 2× `SplitArrangedContainerView` + 2× `NSHostingController.view`); each pane adds another `PaneDragContainerView` + `NSHostingController.view` + `PaneContainerView`.

**Fix candidates:**
- Drop `SplitArrangedContainerView` wrappers; let `NSSplitView`'s `arrangedSubviews` host content directly. Each wrapper exists for `mouseDownCanMoveWindow=false` and `masksToBounds`; both can be set on the hosted view directly or via a subclass.
- Collapse the per-pane `PaneDragContainerView` + nested hosting view chain into a single subclassed hosting view that absorbs the drag-container responsibilities.

**Expected impact (with caveats):** the visible workspace's layout cost is what's left after Phase 1 — currently 700–1,500 ms on heavy switches. AppKit's Auto Layout cost isn't linear in tree depth, but reducing depth by ~30% plausibly shaves 30–50% off that. So Phase 6 might take heavy-switch asyncDone from ~1,500 ms → ~750–1,000 ms. Median (already ~325 ms, dominated by lighter switches) probably barely moves.

**Worth-it threshold:** c11 owns its bonsplit fork, so the upstream-coordination cost is gone — this is purely a "structural risk vs perf gain" decision. Each wrapper exists for a reason (drag handling, layout binding, focus routing); pulling them risks regressions in typing latency, drag-and-drop, focus restoration. **Do not start Phase 6 until Phases 2–4 land and we re-measure.** If heavy-switch tail is still in the 800–1,500 ms range and attributable to bonsplit depth (verifiable in a fresh `sample`), Phase 6 becomes justified. If by then tail is sub-500 ms, skip it.

## Cross-cutting observations

- **Ghostty cost is intrinsic.** Per-surface NSView + Metal layer + renderer thread are baseline. No phase tries to remove these; all phases target architectural cost (SwiftUI cascades, AppKit Auto Layout traversal, deferred binds).
- **Typing latency hot paths must NOT regress.** `TerminalSurface.forceRefresh()`, `TabItemView` equatable + `.equatable()`, `WindowTerminalHostView.hitTest()`. Verified intact through Phase 1; each subsequent phase must preserve.
- **Test method:** for each phase, build with `./scripts/reload.sh --tag <name>`, exercise with realistic workspace state, extract per-phase dt breakdown from `/tmp/c11-debug-<tag>.log` `ws.*` events, compare to baseline.

## Status

- Phase 0 + Phase 1: shipped (PR #127, awaiting review/merge).
- Phase 2: implementation in progress (sub-agent active).
- Phases 3–5: planned, not started.
- Phase 6: gated on Phase 2–4 results.
data.title: Workspace-switch performance umbrella

data.body:
Phase 0 + Phase 1 shipped in https://github.com/Stage-11-Agentics/c11/pull/127 (commit 4ac704b8 on branch perf/workspace-switch-instrumentation). Awaiting review/merge.

Phase 2 implementation in progress in c11 surface tagged `phase2-impl` (pane:52). Upstream eval in surface tagged `upstream-eval` (pane:53), writing memo to /tmp/upstream-eval-c11-perf.md.

# Phase 2 + Phase 3 update + sharpened map for next investigator

**By:** Phase-2/3 implementation agent (sub-agent in pane:52, c11 DEV phase3-instr.app)
**Date:** 2026-05-06

## Phase 2 — shipped (PR #128, merged into PR #127's branch)

**What landed:** defensive guard in `host.onDidMoveToWindow` (GhosttyTerminalView.swift) that skips the portal bind when host bounds are still 0×0. The body of `forceRefresh` already early-returns under those conditions, but binding with zero anchor frame would hide the hosted view and require an async sync to recover with real geometry — a redundant bind+sync cycle on every workspace mount. The guard lets `onGeometryChanged` perform the bind on the first geometry tick once SwiftUI's layout pass has set a real frame.

**What did NOT work:** the original "(a) pre-attach host containers eagerly" approach. I tried it first — installed a per-window prebind parent NSView in the window's contentView and attached the freshly-created HostContainerView to it inside `updateNSView`, expecting SwiftUI to reparent it into its real layer (same window → no `viewDidMoveToWindow` fire → synchronous bind path runs). **It broke terminals entirely.** The dlog showed the host stayed inside the prebind parent permanently — SwiftUI's `NSViewRepresentable` does NOT reparent a returned view that already has a superview. The portal correctly hid the terminal because the anchor's ancestor chain was hidden (prebind was `isHidden=true`). Verdict: approach (a) requires changing the SwiftUI representable contract or the host lifecycle in a way that's deeper than a leaf-level fix. **The branch history (de1af53fc + a9e2040ce in `perf/phase2-portal-bind`) is preserved if anyone wants to revisit.**

**Caveat under load:** in the live trace from the heavy-load test (15 agents, 4 workspaces), the new `ws.hostState.deferBindOnDidMove` event **never fired**. AppKit consistently sized the host before `viewDidMoveToWindow` arrived — meaning under realistic load the bind always ran with a valid frame anyway. Phase 2 is a **defensive guard**, not an active perf win in this build. The "the bind triggers another layout pass" framing in Argus-1's original Phase 2 description didn't reproduce as described; the second cascade hypothesis was wrong (or specific to a load profile we didn't hit).

## Phase 3 — shipped (PR #129, merged into PR #127's branch)

Phase 3 ended up doing what Argus-1 had labeled "Phase 3" only partially. The actual deliverable: comprehensive instrumentation + a small data-validated optimization. The split-by-runloop-tick fix Argus-1 described for Phase 3 is now reframed (see Refined Map below) — `panel.focus()` does cost 140–200 ms but it's NOT the dominant cost.

**What landed:**

1. **Instrumentation (DEBUG-only dlog markers).** New events let the next investigator see every phase of a workspace switch without touching the source:
   - `ws.select.asyncEnter / asyncPostFocusPanel / asyncPostTitleUpdate / asyncPostMarkRead` — phases of the queued async block from `selectedTabId.didSet`.
   - `ws.focusPanel.enter / preFocus / postPanelFocus / postEnsureFocus / exit` — substeps inside `focusSelectedTabPanel`.
   - `terminalPanel.focus.timing` — splits `panel.focus()` into `surface.setFocus` / `hostedView.setActive` / `hostedView.ensureFocus` durations.
   - `portal.bind.timing` — wall-clock `bind()` duration when >5ms.
   - `ws.geometryReconcile.pass` — per-pass duration plus refreshed/skipped panel counts and `needsFollowUp` flag.
   - `ws.layoutFollowUp.attempt` — per-attempt total, flushMs, didMakeProgress, needsMoreWork, stalled count, reason.

2. **Optimization: drop duplicate `layoutSubtreeIfNeeded` loop.** `attemptEventDrivenLayoutFollowUp` already calls `flushWorkspaceWindowLayouts()` at the start of each attempt; `reconcileTerminalGeometryPass()` was doing the exact same loop again. Single caller, safe to drop. **Real but modest saving** (~5–15 ms per switch) — the inner full-window layout pass is sub-millisecond per attempt, much smaller than I expected before measuring.

3. **Optimization: skip `forceRefresh()` for detached / zero-bounds panels.** The body early-returns anyway, but skipping the call entirely avoids the dlog emit and Ghostty C bridge entry per panel during the cascade of follow-up passes that fire while freshly-mounted surfaces settle.

## Refined map (heavy-load measurement, 15 agents, 4 workspaces, post-Phase 3 build)

| phase | ms | notes |
|---|---|---|
| didSet → handoff.complete | 20–24 | Phase 1 ✓ — fully preserved |
| **handoff → asyncEnter** | **880–1450** | **Dominant cost.** Runloop iteration after didSet: SwiftUI body re-eval, 5 surfaces' makeNSView + updateNSView, AppKit lays out the new bonsplit tree, 5 portal binds (35–56 ms total), `splitView.didResize` cascade, Ghostty surface attach + first `forceRefresh`. The async block is queued behind ALL of this. |
| panel.focus() | 140–200 | Three calls (`surface.setFocus`, `hostedView.setActive`, `hostedView.ensureFocus`). Was hidden inside the old "Gap 2". |
| post-focus → asyncDone | ~3 | `updateWindowTitleForSelectedTab` + `markFocusedPanelReadIfActive` are negligible. |
| **layout follow-up attempts** | 4–9 ms each, ~8/switch (~40 ms total) | Each attempt now flushes once instead of twice; `flushWorkspaceWindowLayouts` is <1ms per call. |
| asyncDone total (typical) | ~1050–1650 | Compare to pre-Phase-1 1000–6000 ms median/p95. |

## What this map redirects for the next investigator

**Argus-1's Phase 3** (split the queued async block by runloop tick) is now of marginal value. `panel.focus()` is 140–200 ms but `updateWindowTitleForSelectedTab` and `markFocusedPanelReadIfActive` are <1 ms each, so deferring them past the visible flip would only move 140–200 ms of focus work. Worth doing if it's quick, but it's not the lever.

**The real lever** is the 880–1450 ms `handoff.complete → asyncEnter` gap. This is a **single runloop iteration of synchronous work** triggered by `didSet`: SwiftUI body re-eval + AppKit layout for the new workspace's split tree + portal binds + Ghostty surface attach + forceRefresh. The queued `DispatchQueue.main.async` block can't run until that iteration completes.

Subdivisions to attack (ranked by likely yield):

1. **Argus-1's Phase 4 (narrow `terminalContent`'s ForEach observation).** The biggest potential win remaining. Per-workspace `ObservableObject` slices so non-active workspaces don't re-evaluate their bodies on every `selectedTabId` flip. Cuts SwiftUI body cost from O(mounted) to O(2). Estimated 50–150 ms on heavy switches; possibly more under high mount counts.

2. **Argus-1's Phase 5 (split `TabManager` into smaller stores).** Highest yield for *ambient* sluggishness (separate from switching). Every `TabManager` `@Published` change currently broadcasts to 4+ root views. Splitting reduces broadcast volume 5–10×. **Strongly recommended after Phase 4** — they reinforce each other.

3. **Argus-1's Phase 6 (bonsplit nesting reduction).** Gated on Phases 4-5 first, per Argus-1's note. The 30-level NSView depth is the load-bearing structural cost, but pulling it carries real risk to drag/focus/typing-latency. Verify with a fresh `sample` after 4-5 land before committing.

4. **NEW: investigate the SwiftUI mount cost itself.** Instrumentation now shows 5 SwiftUI updates spread over ~700 ms with 50–260 ms gaps between them (each surface's mount waits for AppKit layout before the next can run). One angle: render WorkspaceContentView's panes via a custom container that batches all surfaces into a single body pass. Speculative, may need profiling.

## What's NOT a productive lever (now confirmed by data)

- **Bind coalescer.** Originally on the Phase 3b shortlist. Bind work is only 35–56 ms total per switch; coalescing wouldn't move the needle.
- **`reconcileTerminalGeometryPass` chain.** Initially suspected to be a multi-hundred-ms cost. Actually 4–9 ms per attempt × ~8 attempts = ~40 ms. Already reduced ~half by dropping the duplicate flush.
- **Approach (a) prebind via SwiftUI return value.** Confirmed broken (SwiftUI doesn't reparent already-superviewed NSViews). Don't try it again from the same angle.

## Tagged build for verification

`c11 DEV phase3-instr.app` (DerivedData/c11-phase3-instr) has all the instrumentation live. Tag stays usable for incremental rebuilds in future phases unless deliberately pruned.

## Anomaly to flag

In the heavy-load trace, one switch (id=5, D8B81→751DE) clocked in at 4449 ms — 3× normal. The operator noted system-level slowdown (voice-to-text animation lag) at the same time. Likely external system pressure, not c11. If it shows up in clean isolation on a future build, worth investigating. Not blocking.

## Status update

- Phase 0 + Phase 1: shipped, in PR #127.
- Phase 2: shipped, defensive guard, no measurable improvement under load — see caveat above.
- Phase 3: shipped (instrumentation + dedupe), small saving + map for next investigator.
- Phases 4–5: **recommended next**. Most of the remaining latency lives there.
- Phase 6: still gated on 4-5 results.

provenance.reason: Phase 2 + 3 shipped; sharpened map for next investigator

## Problem

| 1,530 ms | `ws.view.selectedChange` (SwiftUI body cascade fully drained) |
| 1,672 ms | last `ws.swiftui.update` for surfaces |
| ~3,668 ms | `ws.unfocus.defer` — silent gap = AppKit layout cascade #1 |
| ~5,118 ms | `ws.select.asyncDone` — silent gap = AppKit layout cascade #2 |

### Phase 4 — Narrow `terminalContent`'s ForEach observation
Phase 1 stops AppKit from laying out invisible workspaces, but the SwiftUI body of every mounted `WorkspaceContentView` still re-evaluates on every `selectedTabId` flip. If by then tail is sub-500 ms, skip it.

- **Ghostty cost is intrinsic.** Per-surface NSView + Metal layer + renderer thread are baseline. No phase tries to remove these; all phases target architectural cost (SwiftUI cascades, AppKit Auto Layout traversal, deferred binds).
- **Typing latency hot paths must NOT regress.** `TerminalSurface.forceRefresh()`, `TabItemView` equatable + `.equatable()`, `WindowTerminalHostView.hitTest()`. Verified intact through Phase 1; each subsequent phase must preserve.

## Status

- Phase 0 + Phase 1: shipped (PR #127, awaiting review/merge).
- Phase 2: implementation in progress (sub-agent active).
- Phases 3–5: planned, not started.
- Phase 6: gated on Phase 2–4 results.
data.to:
# Workspace-switch performance — umbrella roadmap

## Status (2026-05-06)

**Shipped via PR #127 (Phases 0 + 1 + 2 + 3, squashed onto `perf/workspace-switch-instrumentation`):**

- **Phase 0** — `os_signpost` instrumentation + Sentry breadcrumb `workspace.switch.complete`.
- **Phase 1** — `AppKitHiddenWrapper` makes off-screen workspaces invisible to AppKit's layout walk. Big win: `handoff.start` 1500ms → 17–77ms, `asyncDone` p95 6000ms → 1200ms, 90s tail eliminated.
- **Phase 2** — defensive guard skipping the portal bind when host bounds are 0×0 in `viewDidMoveToWindow`. Benign under load (the guard never fires); kept as safety. The originally-proposed "pre-attach host containers eagerly" approach was tried and confirmed broken — SwiftUI's `NSViewRepresentable` does NOT reparent a returned view that already has a superview. Don't try it again from that angle.
- **Phase 3** — comprehensive instrumentation (`ws.select.async*`, `ws.focusPanel.*`, `terminalPanel.focus.timing`, `portal.bind.timing`, `ws.geometryReconcile.pass`, `ws.layoutFollowUp.attempt`) + dropped duplicate `layoutSubtreeIfNeeded` in `reconcileTerminalGeometryPass` + skip `forceRefresh()` for detached/zero-bounds panels. Saving ~5–15ms; real value is the map.

## Refined cost map (heavy-load measurement, 15 agents / 4 workspaces, post-Phase 3)

| phase | ms | notes |
|---|---|---|
| didSet → handoff.complete | 20–24 | Phase 1 ✓ |
| **handoff → asyncEnter** | **880–1450** | **Dominant remaining cost.** Single runloop iteration triggered by didSet: SwiftUI body re-eval, 5 surfaces' `makeNSView` + `updateNSView`, AppKit lays out the new bonsplit tree, 5 portal binds (35–56ms), `splitView.didResize` cascade, surface attach + first `forceRefresh`. The async block is queued behind ALL of this. |
| panel.focus() | 140–200 | `surface.setFocus` + `hostedView.setActive` + `hostedView.ensureFocus`. |
| post-focus → asyncDone | ~3 | `updateWindowTitleForSelectedTab` + `markFocusedPanelReadIfActive` are negligible. |
| layout follow-up attempts | 4–9ms each, ~8/switch (~40ms total) | After Phase 3's flush dedup. `flushWorkspaceWindowLayouts` is <1ms per call. |
| asyncDone total (typical) | ~1050–1650 | Compare to pre-Phase-1 1000–6000ms median/p95. |

## Confirmed dead ends (don't re-run these experiments)

- **Pre-attach host containers via SwiftUI return value** — SwiftUI doesn't reparent already-superviewed NSViews. Branch history preserved in `de1af53fc + a9e2040ce` on `perf/phase2-portal-bind`.
- **Bind coalescer** — bind work is only 35–56ms total; coalescing wouldn't move the needle.
- **`reconcileTerminalGeometryPass` chain attack** — only ~40ms total per switch. Already trimmed in Phase 3.

## What's next

The dominant 880–1450ms `handoff.complete → asyncEnter` gap is a single synchronous runloop iteration. Until it's broken up or shrunk, the queued `DispatchQueue.main.async` block can't run sooner. Subdivisions to attack, ranked by yield:

### Phase 4 — Narrow `terminalContent`'s ForEach observation (HIGHEST YIELD NEXT)

Phase 1 stops AppKit from laying out invisible workspaces, but the SwiftUI body of every mounted `WorkspaceContentView` still re-evaluates on every `selectedTabId` flip. The `ForEach(mountedWorkspaces)` in `ContentView.terminalContent` re-creates prop bindings (`isWorkspaceVisible`, `isWorkspaceInputActive`, `workspacePortalPriority`) for each mounted workspace whenever any of them changes. So 7 mounted workspaces' bodies + their pane trees + every surface's `updateNSView` all re-evaluate per switch.

**Fix:** hoist per-workspace presentation state into per-workspace `ObservableObject`s (e.g., `WorkspacePresentationStore`). Each `WorkspaceContentView` observes only its own slice. The ForEach iteration only varies on workspace identity; props don't change. Workspaces whose state didn't change skip body re-eval, skip `updateNSView`, skip the whole subtree's SwiftUI cascade.

**Expected impact:** cuts the SwiftUI body-eval cascade from O(mounted) to O(2). Probably 50–150ms on heavy switches; possibly more under high mount counts.

**Effort/risk:** Medium / medium. Localized to ContentView + WorkspaceContentView. State propagation must preserve handoff-during-retiring and background-prime semantics.

### Phase 5 — Narrow `@EnvironmentObject TabManager` into smaller stores

`TabManager` is held as `@EnvironmentObject` in ~4 root views. Any `@Published` change — `selectedTabId`, `tabs`, `isWorkspaceCycleHot`, `pendingBackgroundWorkspaceLoadIds`, status mutations, history mutations — re-evaluates **all four** root views. Structural reason ambient activity feels heavy even when nothing visible is changing.

**Fix:** split `TabManager`'s published surface into smaller `ObservableObject`s — `WorkspaceSelectionStore` (`selectedTabId`), `TabsListStore` (`tabs`), `WorkspaceStatusStore` (status/badges), `WorkspaceCycleStore` (cycle/hot state). Each root view observes only the slice it needs.

**Expected impact:** the single biggest fix for *ambient* "system feels sluggish" (separate from switching). Likely cuts SwiftUI body-eval volume by 5–10× under heavy agent activity. Strongly recommended after Phase 4 — they reinforce each other.

**Effort/risk:** High / medium. `TabManager` is 3,000+ lines; careful refactor, not a fix. Mechanical (move `@Published` markers, introduce sub-stores, thread through environment) — no new logic. Spread over 2–3 PRs.

### Phase 6 — Bonsplit nesting reduction (gated on 4–5 results)

The 30-level `_layoutSubtreeWithOldSize:` recursion comes from bonsplit. Each split level adds ~5 NSView wrappers (`NSSplitView` + 2× `SplitArrangedContainerView` + 2× `NSHostingController.view`); each pane adds another `PaneDragContainerView` + `NSHostingController.view` + `PaneContainerView`.

**Fix candidates:**
- Drop `SplitArrangedContainerView` wrappers; let `NSSplitView`'s `arrangedSubviews` host content directly.
- Collapse the per-pane `PaneDragContainerView` + nested hosting view chain into a single subclassed hosting view.

**Expected impact (with caveats):** AppKit Auto Layout cost isn't linear in tree depth, but reducing depth ~30% plausibly shaves 30–50% off the visible-workspace layout cost. Could take heavy-switch asyncDone from ~1500ms → ~750–1000ms.

**Worth-it threshold:** **Do not start until Phases 4–5 land and we re-measure.** If heavy-switch tail is still ≥800ms and attributable to bonsplit depth (verifiable in a fresh `sample`), Phase 6 becomes justified. If by then tail is <500ms, skip it.

**Effort/risk:** High. Each wrapper exists for a reason (drag handling, layout binding, focus routing); pulling them risks regressions in typing latency, drag-and-drop, focus restoration.

### Phase 7 (NEW from Phase 3 findings) — Investigate SwiftUI mount cost itself

Instrumentation now shows 5 SwiftUI updates spread over ~700ms with 50–260ms gaps between them — each surface's mount waits for AppKit layout before the next can run. Speculative angle: render WorkspaceContentView's panes via a custom container that batches surfaces into a single body pass. May need profiling first.

## Cross-cutting constraints

- **Ghostty cost is intrinsic.** Per-surface NSView + Metal layer + renderer thread are baseline. No phase tries to remove these.
- **Typing-latency hot paths must NOT regress.** `TerminalSurface.forceRefresh()`, `TabItemView` equatable + `.equatable()`, `WindowTerminalHostView.hitTest()`. Verified intact through Phases 0–3.
- **Test method:** build with `./scripts/reload.sh --tag <name>`, exercise with realistic workspace state, extract per-phase dt breakdown from `/tmp/c11-debug-<tag>.log` `ws.*` events, compare to Phase 3 baseline. Tagged build `c11 DEV phase3-instr.app` is ready for incremental work.

## Anomaly to flag

In the heavy-load trace, one switch clocked at 4449ms — 3× normal. Operator noted simultaneous voice-to-text animation lag; likely external system pressure, not c11. If reproducible in clean isolation on a future build, worth investigating. Not blocking.
